1.1: An oracle against Nineveh. What Nahum of Elkosh saw.
1.2: Yahweh is a jealous God — and he avenges. Hear it three times: Yahweh avenges his adversaries; Yahweh reserves wrath for his enemies. He is not indifferent.
1.3: Slow to anger — but immense in power. Yahweh will never simply acquit the guilty; that patience is not weakness. His road runs through hurricane and tempest; the storm clouds trail behind him like dust from his footfall.
1.4: He rebukes the sea — and it dries up. Every river drains at his command. The lush uplands of Bashan go brown; Carmel's crest withers; Lebanon's flowers drop. Fertility itself retreats before him.
1.5: Mountains quake when he draws near; hills dissolve. The whole earth convulses before his face — land and every living soul. Nothing holds still.
1.6: Who keeps their footing when his wrath breaks out? Who survives the burning of his anger? It floods like fire; it shatters stone. Nothing stands.
1.7: But the LORD is good. When trouble comes, he is the fortress. He knows every one who trusts him — knows them by name.
1.8: Against Nineveh he unleashes a flood that sweeps the place entirely clean; and his enemies he chases into the blackest dark.
1.9: Whatever scheme you hatch against Yahweh — he ends it completely. He will not have to act twice. One blow is enough.
1.10: They are a tangle of thornbush, and drunk besides — and they will burn like bone-dry straw. Gone in a flash.
1.11: From your midst, Nineveh, came the one who schemed against Yahweh — the architect of disaster.
1.12: This is what Yahweh says: No matter how strong and numerous they stand — they will be sheared down and swept away. I put you through the fire, Judah. But I am done. The punishment is over.
1.13: And now — Yahweh acts: the yoke cracks, the chains snap. You are free.
1.14: Yahweh has pronounced his sentence on Nineveh: the name is finished — no descendant will carry it forward. His idols — destroyed. His temple — emptied. His grave — already dug. Contemptible beyond recovery.
1.15: Look! There on the mountains — a runner, bringing the good news of peace! Celebrate, Judah! Keep your festivals; pay what you vowed. Nineveh is finished. The one who trampled you will never march through again — cut off, utterly, forever.

2.1: The Smasher has come. Post every defender on the walls; watch every road; gird yourselves; pour out every last ounce of strength. (For all the good it will do.)
2.2: For Yahweh is reclaiming the honor of Jacob and restoring Israel's dignity — because the strippers stripped them bare and wrecked every branch of their vine. Nineveh's fall is Jacob's restoration.
2.3: Blood-red shields; soldiers clad in scarlet. Chariots catching the light like torches on the day of battle. Spears of cypress raised and trembling — ready to fall.
2.4: Chariots screaming through the streets, crashing through the squares — flashing past like torches, vanishing like lightning. The city is consumed in its own panic.
2.5: He calls his officers — but they trip over each other scrambling to the walls. Too late. The enemy's siege shield is already going up outside.
2.6: The river floodgates burst. The palace itself is swept away.
2.7: The sentence is final. The queen of Nineveh is stripped and led away. Her attendants wail like doves — beating their chests — and follow her into captivity.
2.8: Nineveh is a draining pool — her people streaming out like water spilling from a cracked vessel. They shout, 'Stop! Stand and fight!' Not one looks back.
2.9: Take the silver! Take the gold! Centuries of tribute piled up — no end to it. Treasure from every storehouse in the world, looted there and now looted again.
2.10: Empty. Hollow. Destroyed. Every heart dissolves; every knee buckles; every belly cramps with dread; every face goes ashen. This is Nineveh, stripped to nothing.
2.11: Where is the lion's den now? Where the great pride gathered — the old lion, the lioness, the cubs — and the whole earth trembled to approach? Gone. As if it never was.
2.12: Not long ago the lion fed the whole pride from the kill — tore and strangled without restraint. The dens ran with blood; the lairs were stacked with the flesh of nations. Nations that no longer exist.
2.13: Here is the verdict of Yahweh of armies: I am against you, Nineveh. Your war-machine burns. Your soldiers — devoured. Your empire of stolen wealth — ended. Your ambassadors will never again be heard giving orders to anyone.

3.1: Woe to the blood-soaked city! Built on lies, gorged with plunder — the killing never stops.
3.2: Hear it — the whip cracking! The wheels thundering! Horses at full gallop; chariots lurching and crashing through the streets!
3.3: Cavalry charging. Swords catching the light. Spears everywhere. The dead lie in heaps — too many to count. The living trip over those who just fell.
3.4: Because of the endless seductions of Nineveh — graceful, lethal, a sorceress — luring nations into ruin, enslaving whole peoples with her dark arts and imperial sorcery —
3.5: This is what Yahweh of armies says: I am against you, Nineveh. I will strip you bare before the watching nations. The humiliation you dealt out — you will receive it in full public view.
3.6: I will cover you in filth. I will make you an object of contempt. Every nation will come to stare at what has become of you.
3.7: Everyone who passes will flinch and turn away: 'Nineveh — destroyed. Who weeps for her?' And there will be no one. No mourners. No comforters. No one at all.
3.8: Are you greater than Thebes — great No-Amon — encircled by the Nile, the sea itself as her fortress wall? She thought herself invincible too. Do you not remember what happened to her?
3.9: Her coalition was massive — Ethiopia, Egypt, unlimited in numbers; Libya and the western peoples fighting at her side. Every great power of the known world stood with Thebes.
3.10: And yet Thebes fell. Carried away in chains. Her infants smashed on the cobblestones at every street corner. Her nobles auctioned off by lot. Her great men dragged away in irons. This happened — to a city mightier than you, Nineveh.
3.11: You too will reel like a drunk man. You too will vanish into hiding. You too will be begging for someone to rescue you. And no one will come.
3.12: Your fortresses — every one — are overripe figs hanging loose. One shake and they drop straight into your enemy's open mouth.
3.13: Look at your defenders — they have no fight left in them. Your city gates stand open; your enemies walk straight in. The bars that should have held them are already ash.
3.14: Get your water stored! Reinforce every wall! Get into the mud — tread the clay — man the brickworks and build! Build, build! None of it will save you.
3.15: In those very preparations fire will find you. The sword will finish what the fire starts. You'll be eaten like a field of grain in a locust swarm. So yes — multiply like locusts! What does it matter? The devourer is already coming.
3.16: Your merchants outnumbered the stars — trading across the whole world, enriching you with the wealth of nations. But they are like locusts: they strip the land bare, then lift their wings and vanish. In your day of disaster, not one will stay.
3.17: Your generals, your palace officials — like grasshoppers sunning themselves on a cold wall. Let the sun get hot and they vanish. Gone. No one will find them.
3.18: Your generals sleep in death, O king of Assyria. Your nobles lie face-down in the dust. Your people are scattered across every mountain and valley — and not a shepherd left to find them.
3.19: No cure for what has come upon you, Nineveh. The wound is mortal. When the news breaks, every nation will clap — not in grief but in relief. For who among them has not felt the lash of your endless cruelty?
